concepts.name: Recursion
concepts.description: A method of solving problems where a function calls itself within its own code to solve smaller versions of the same problem

subconcepts.name: Tail recursion
subconcepts.description: A specific form of recursion where the recursive call is the last operation in the function, allowing for optimization by the compiler or interpreter

subconcepts.name: Mutual recursion
subconcepts.description: A situation where two or more functions call each other in a cyclic manner, often used to solve problems that can be broken down into smaller, similar subproblems

subconcepts.name: Wrapper recursion
subconcepts.description: A form of recursion that employs a wrapper function that is directly invoked but does not recurse itself. Instead, it calls a separate auxiliary function that actually performs the recursion

concepts.name: Backtracking
concepts.description: A general algorithmic technique that incrementally builds candidates to the solutions and abandons a candidate as soon as it determines that the candidate cannot possibly lead to a valid solution

concepts.name: Branch and bound
concepts.description: A  method for solving optimization problems by breaking them down into smaller subproblems and using a bounding function to eliminate subproblems that cannot contain the optimal solution

concepts.name: Brute-force search
concepts.description: A very general problem-solving technique and algorithmic paradigm that consists of systematically checking all possible candidates for whether or not each candidate satisfies the problem's statement

concepts.name: Divide and conquer
concepts.description: A method that breaks down a problem into two or more sub-problems of the same or related type, until these become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then combined to give a solution to the original problem.

concepts.name: Dynamic programming
concepts.description: A method for solving complex problems by breaking them down into simpler subproblems, storing the results of those subproblems to avoid redundant work

subconcepts.name: Memoization
subconcepts.description: An optimization technique used primarily to speed up computer programs by storing the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again

subconcepts.name: Tabulation
subconcepts.description: A bottom-up approach to dynamic programming that involves filling in a table (usually an array) based on the results of smaller subproblems, starting from the base cases and working upwards

concepts.name: Greedy algorithm
concepts.description: A algorithm that, at each step, makes the choice that is locally optimal, and subsequently does not reconsider past choices.

concepts.name: Randomized algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm that employs a degree of randomness as part of its logic, or procedure

subconcepts.name: Las Vegas algorithm
subconcepts.description: A randomized algorithm that always produces a correct result, or informs the user of failure (e.g. Quicksort )

subconcepts.name: Monte Carlo algorithm
subconcepts.description: A randomized algorithm whose output may be incorrect with a certain (typically small) probability.

concepts.name: No algorithmic paradigm
concepts.description: Use when the code does not implement a recognizable algorithmic problem-solving strategy beyond ordinary syntax, idioms, or business logic.
